story:
It’s my first time on a plane, alone and already flustered about where to sit and what to do with my bag, when a kind-eyed old man beside me gently points to my seat, B3. I’m ready to cry, but his calm saves me. Everything on this aircraft feels cramped and confusing, and I’m awkwardly juggling my bag until he says I can set it on the empty seat between us. He bought both seats for the extra space, he says with a wink. He’s tall with silky gray hair and the standard grandpa glasses, but it’s his eyes that stand out—warm, steady, like he’d know exactly what to say. He smells faintly of cinnamon and aftershave, and his dimples show because he seems to smile all the time. I admit it’s my first flight and I feel like a fish out of water. He chuckles that every first becomes a good story and tells me his name is Earl. If I have any questions, ask.

Before I can stop myself, I blurt, “What is love?” and immediately wish I could reel the words back in. I just met this man, he helped me find my seat, and now I’m asking him about the biggest thing there is. But the question has been rattling around my head ever since my nine-year relationship imploded. I’m here because he broke my heart so thoroughly it feels beyond repair, and I needed a fresh start. I apologize, bracing for the awkwardness.

Earl doesn’t flinch. “Do you remember how excited you were on Christmas morning?” he asks, staring down at something in his hand. I tell him yes—checking for crumbs on the plate of cookies, the carrots for the reindeer, the thrill of it. He nods. “Love is getting that feeling every time you look at them. Waking up next to them, eating with them, laughing with them—your normal day feels like the gift you’ve been asking for all year, and you finally get it. It’s exhilarating.” He’s holding a worn charm bracelet, and it’s clear he’s not just looking at jewelry but at a memory. I tell him it’s a beautiful way to see love and say I hope I find my Christmas morning someday. We’ve been in the air for a bit; the view is all blue sky and glimmers of water far below. He smiles, says we all have a Christmas morning out there, and that he was lucky to spend forty years with his.

He asks where I’m headed. I tell him I’m moving to North Carolina for a job at a publishing company—no more strings to my small town, just a sandy beach and an ocean breeze I hope will put me back together. He wishes that breeze gives me everything I hope it does. I picture myself under an umbrella with a book, waves rolling in, sun on my skin, families laughing nearby. I decide the ocean will cure my heartbreak.

I tell Earl I’m going to nap so I have energy when we land. “No problem, kiddo,” he says, winking. “You have a new chapter to write.” I close my eyes, smiling. See you soon, fresh start.
